questions.question: Explain the Bully Algorithm for leader election with an example where process 1 initiates election after coordinator failure.
keyPoints: Process 1 sends election messages to all processes with higher IDs
Processes 2,3,4,5 respond with OK messages
Process 1 waits for OK from processes 2-5
If no OK received, process with highest ID becomes coordinator
The new coordinator broadcasts coordinator message to all

questions.question: Describe the Ring Algorithm for leader election. How does it handle process failures?
keyPoints: Processes are arranged in a logical ring
Election message circulates clockwise collecting IDs
When message returns to initiator, highest ID becomes leader
On failure detection, process bypasses failed neighbor
Ring is reconfigured to exclude failed node

questions.question: Compare the Bully Algorithm and Ring Algorithm in terms of message complexity and fault tolerance.
keyPoints: Bully: O(n²) messages worst case, Ring: O(n) messages
Bully requires synchronous communication, Ring more tolerant
Bully uses broadcast, Ring uses token passing
Ring handles failures by reconfiguring ring
Bully can have cascading elections on failures

questions.question: What are the assumptions made by the Bully Algorithm for correct operation?
keyPoints: All processes know each other's process IDs
Processes can send messages directly to any other process
Message delivery is guaranteed (no loss)
System is synchronous or timeout mechanism exists for failure detection
Exactly one leader exists at any time (invariant)

questions.question: Explain the role of the coordinator in distributed systems and why leader election is critical.
keyPoints: Coordinator manages distributed operations and transactions
Ensures consistency in distributed databases
Handles concurrency control and lock management
Acts as single point of coordination
Leader election ensures system continuity after failures

questions.question: Describe the message types used in the Bully Algorithm and their purposes.
keyPoints: ELECTION message: Initiates leader election process
OK message: Response indicating sender is alive
COORDINATOR message: Announces newly elected leader
KILL message: Informs old leader it has been replaced
All messages carry sender process ID

questions.question: Explain the Bully Algorithm in detail with a diagram showing processes P1-P5. If coordinator P5 fails, trace the election process when P1 detects the failure.
keyPoints: Diagram showing 5 processes with IDs 1-5
P1 detects failure via timeout, initiates election
P1 sends election messages to P2,P3,P4,P5
P2,P3,P4 respond with OK messages
P1 waits, then P4 (highest) initiates election
P4 becomes coordinator, broadcasts COORDINATOR message
Explain each step with message flow
Message complexity discussion

questions.question: Discuss the Ring Algorithm for leader election. With an example of 6 processes in a ring, trace the election when process P3 fails.
keyPoints: Logical ring arrangement of processes
Election message starts from one point
Message collects IDs as it circulates
Highest ID becomes leader when message returns
P3 failure detection by P2 (neighbor)
Ring reconfiguration: P2 connects to P4
New election initiated if needed
Advantages over Bully Algorithm

questions.question: Compare and contrast the Bully Algorithm and Ring Algorithm for leader election in distributed systems.
keyPoints: Message complexity: O(n²) vs O(n)
Communication pattern: broadcast vs token passing
Synchronous requirements
Failure handling mechanisms
Scalability considerations
When to use each algorithm
Practical applications
Limitations of each approach

questions.question: Design a leader election solution for a distributed database system with 8 server nodes. Explain how your solution handles concurrent elections and network partitions.
keyPoints: Use priority-based election with server ID as priority
Election initiated on coordinator failure detection
Quorum-based validation to prevent split-brain
Handling concurrent elections with unique election IDs
Recovery from network partitions
Heartbeat mechanism for failure detection
Coordinator message propagation
State synchronization after election
